threats.title: forceInclusion Missing Timestamp-Based Delay Check Allows Premature Force Inclusion
threats.hypothesis_description: The forceInclusion function only enforces a block-number-based delay check but entirely omits the time-based delay check. The error ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon is defined in Error.sol but never referenced anywhere. The delaySeconds variable is stored and used in getTimeBounds but NOT validated during force inclusion. This means force inclusion is gated solely by block count, allowing messages to be force-included before the intended wall-clock time delay has elapsed.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect: an error type was created for this exact check, the delay parameter is stored, but the enforcement code was never written. On chains where block production rate varies (faster block times during high activity), the block threshold is reached before the intended time-based threshold, creating a window for premature force inclusion.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Only block check present: 'if (l1BlockAndTime[0] + delayBlocks_ >= block.number) revert ForceIncludeBlockTooSoon()' — no corresponding timestamp check using delaySeconds and l1BlockAndTime[1]. Error ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon defined in Error.sol but never used.

preconditions: isDelayBufferable or standard delay mode active
Delayed messages exist that have passed the block delay but not the time delay
Block production rate is faster than the assumed rate used to calibrate delayBlocks vs delaySeconds

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: forceInclusion checks l1BlockAndTime[0] + delayBlocks_ < block.number but never checks l1BlockAndTime[1] + delaySeconds_ < block.timestamp
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: During periods of fast block production, block threshold is reached before time threshold, allowing premature force inclusion
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can force-include messages before the sequencer's intended exclusivity window expires, disrupting batch posting and potentially causing L2 reorgs

economic_questions: What is the typical ratio of delayBlocks to delaySeconds in production deployments?
How much does L1 block time vary in practice, and what's the maximum divergence?
What economic damage can premature force inclusion cause to the sequencer or users?
validation_requirements: Verify ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon is never referenced in the entire codebase
Analyze historical L1 block time variance to quantify premature inclusion window
Test force inclusion with l1BlockAndTime[1] that would fail a time check

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas cost for forceInclusion call (~0.01 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: disruption value depends on sequencer operations and pending L2 state

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During periods of unusually fast L1 block production
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could disrupt sequencer ordering if force inclusion happens before intended time window

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Sequencer operations, L2 users depending on ordering guarantees
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Potential L2 reorg during premature force inclusion window
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Depends on transactions in the affected ordering window

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Compare delayBlocks and delaySeconds values in production configs
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Calculate maximum premature inclusion window under varying block times

threats.title: Staking Pool FCFS Withdrawal Race After Assertion/Edge Slashing Enables Deposit Theft
threats.hypothesis_description: AbsBoldStakingPool tracks individual depositBalance per depositor, but this balance is never reduced when the pool's tokens are transferred to the rollup/challengeManager via createAssertion/createEdge. If the staked assertion or edge loses and the stake is slashed, the pool's token balance drops below the sum of depositBalances. Withdrawals are first-come-first-served (FCFS), allowing early withdrawers to claim tokens that should be proportionally shared among all depositors, effectively stealing from later withdrawers.
threats.investigation_rationale: Phase A security analysis flagged this as SWC-105 pattern. The depositBalance mapping never accounts for slashing losses. When pool token balance < sum(depositBalance), the withdrawal race creates unfair distribution. The pool design comments acknowledge excess deposits can be withdrawn early but assume 'all depositors will still be eventually refunded' - which is only true if the assertion wins.

proof_of_signal.pattern: depositBalance[msg.sender] += amount on deposit, depositBalance[msg.sender] = balance - amount on withdrawal, but NO reduction of depositBalance when tokens leave pool via createAssertion/createEdge

preconditions: Pool has excess deposits beyond the required stake amount
The assertion/edge the pool staked on loses (gets slashed)
Multiple depositors exist in the same pool

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: After slashing: pool.balance < sum(depositBalance[*]). First withdrawer gets full depositBalance; later withdrawers get nothing.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Monitor pool's assertion/edge status; if losing, race to withdraw before other depositors
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Early withdrawer captures tokens proportionally belonging to all depositors. Profit = excess_tokens_captured - own_fair_share

economic_questions: How often do pools accumulate excess deposits beyond the required stake?
What is the typical window between slashing and depositor awareness?
Can the withdrawal race be automated via contract monitoring?
validation_requirements: Simulate pool with 150% of required stake, slash 100%, verify FCFS outcome
Verify no mechanism exists to reduce depositBalance on slashing
Check if rollup returnOldDeposit returns partial amounts after rival confirmation

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to the full excess deposit amount in the pool
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Variable, up to 100% of excess deposits if first withdrawer

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Other depositors in the same staking pool
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to 100% of their deposit in worst case
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total excess deposits in pools with losing assertions/edges

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: requiredStake vs typical total pool deposits
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: End-to-end test: deposit → create → slash → race → verify imbalance

threats.title: Strategic forceInclusion Calls Manipulate Delay Buffer State for Subsequent Operations
threats.hypothesis_description: The permissionless forceInclusion function proactively calls buffer.update(l1BlockAndTime[0]) before the delay check when isDelayBufferable is true. This resets buffer.prevBlockNumber and buffer.prevSequencedBlockNumber, directly affecting subsequent buffer depletion/replenishment calculations for all batch posters. An attacker can strategically choose which delayed messages to force-include to manipulate the buffer state, potentially depleting the buffer faster or resetting the sync window.
threats.investigation_rationale: buffer.update modifies global buffer state that affects force inclusion deadlines for ALL future messages. Since forceInclusion is callable by anyone and the message's block number directly inputs the buffer calculation, an attacker can choose old delayed messages to maximize buffer depletion or reset reference points. INV-STATE-006 identifies this as the buffer update being manipulable by arbitrary callers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: buffer.update(l1BlockAndTime[0]) called before delay check in forceInclusion. User-controlled l1BlockAndTime[0] from a valid delayed message directly modifies global buffer state (prevBlockNumber, prevSequencedBlockNumber).

preconditions: isDelayBufferable is true
Multiple delayed messages exist that haven't been force-included
Buffer is not at maximum (can be depleted)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: calcBuffer uses (sequenced - start) as delay and (end - start) as elapsed. Force-including old messages with large delay relative to elapsed can deplete buffer faster.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Choose delayed message with maximally old block number to maximize unexpectedDelay in calcBuffer, depleting buffer toward threshold
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Depleted buffer shortens force inclusion window for subsequent messages, enabling faster force inclusion or disrupting sequencer operations

economic_questions: How much buffer can be depleted by a single strategic force inclusion?
Can sequential force inclusions cascade buffer depletion?
What is the cost to an attacker vs disruption caused?
validation_requirements: Simulate buffer state before and after strategic force inclusion
Calculate maximum buffer depletion from available delayed messages
Test if buffer depletion enables earlier force inclusion of subsequent messages

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: value of disrupting sequencer or enabling premature force inclusion

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When delayed message queue has messages with high block delays
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could shorten force inclusion window, disrupting sequencer ordering guarantees

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Sequencer operations, all L2 users relying on ordering guarantees
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Potential transaction reordering during shortened force inclusion window
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Depends on sequencer operations and pending transactions

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compute calcBuffer output for various forceInclusion scenarios

threats.title: RollupAdminLogic Admin Functions Lack Explicit Access Control Modifiers
threats.hypothesis_description: RollupAdminLogic's externally callable admin functions (setOutbox, removeOldOutbox, setDelayedInbox, pause, resume, setValidator, setOwner, setBaseStake, setInbox, setValidatorWhitelistDisabled, setChallengeManager, etc.) have NO explicit onlyOwner or access control modifiers. The code comment states: 'Functions are only to reach this logic contract if the caller is the owner so there is no need for a redundant onlyOwner check.' This relies entirely on the custom AdminFallbackProxy routing to restrict access. If the proxy routing mechanism has any flaw or is bypassed during an upgrade, all admin functions become permissionless.
threats.investigation_rationale: The RollupProxy uses a custom AdminFallbackProxy (not the standard OZ TransparentUpgradeableProxy), increasing the risk of routing bugs. Functions like setOwner, setChallengeManager, setOutbox are CRITICAL and could drain all staked funds if called by an attacker. Defense-in-depth principles require explicit access control even when proxy routing is expected to enforce it.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Comment: 'Functions are only to reach this logic contract if the caller is the owner so there is no need for a redundant onlyOwner check.' Functions like setOutbox, setOwner have no modifier — completely unguarded at the contract level.

preconditions: A flaw exists in AdminFallbackProxy routing that allows non-admin calls to reach RollupAdminLogic
OR during a proxy upgrade, the routing is temporarily misconfigured
OR the admin logic contract is called directly (not via proxy)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No access control check anywhere in the function bodies for critical admin operations
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: If proxy routing is bypassed, call setOwner to take over rollup, then setChallengeManager to a malicious contract to bypass challenges, then forceConfirmAssertion with attacker-controlled state
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Complete rollup takeover: drain all staked funds via manipulated outbox/bridge, confirm malicious L2 state

economic_questions: Is the AdminFallbackProxy implementation provably correct for all caller scenarios?
Can delegatecall chains from other contracts bypass proxy routing?
During BOLDUpgradeAction, is there a moment where routing is misconfigured?
validation_requirements: Audit AdminFallbackProxy for routing correctness under all caller types
Test calling RollupAdminLogic functions directly (not through proxy)
Verify proxy routing during upgrade transition states

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas for admin function calls if routing is bypassed
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All staked funds + complete L2 state manipulation
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single transaction once proxy routing is bypassed

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During or after proxy upgrades when routing might be misconfigured

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Fuzz test proxy routing with various msg.sender values

threats.title: First Edge Stake Permanently Locked in EdgeChallengeManager When First Edge Loses
threats.hypothesis_description: In createLayerZeroEdge, the first edge in a rival group has its stake transferred to the EdgeChallengeManager contract (address(this)), while subsequent rival stakes go to excessStakeReceiver. The refundStake function requires the edge to be confirmed (via setRefunded check). If the first edge loses (is not confirmed), its stake is permanently locked in the EdgeChallengeManager with no recovery mechanism. Over many challenges, significant funds accumulate as permanently locked tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The stake routing creates asymmetric outcomes: first-edge stakes stay in contract, rival stakes go to excessStakeReceiver. The losing first-edge's stake has no withdrawal path since refundStake requires confirmation. There is no admin function to recover these funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: address receiver = edgeAdded.hasRival ? excessStakeReceiver : address(this); — first edge stake held in contract. refundStake at line 435-443 requires edge.setRefunded() which checks confirmation status.

preconditions: A challenge exists where the first edge created is not the winning edge
stakeToken and stakeAmounts are non-zero
No admin recovery mechanism exists for stuck funds

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Per challenge where first edge loses: stakeAmounts[level] tokens permanently locked
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Natural protocol operation causes fund accumulation; malicious party can intentionally create first edges knowing they'll lose
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct attacker profit, but systematic fund lockup degrades protocol solvency over time

economic_questions: How much stake is locked per losing first-edge?
What is the expected frequency of first-edge losses over protocol lifetime?
Is there an admin upgrade path to recover stuck funds?
validation_requirements: Create two rival edges, confirm the second, verify first's stake is irrecoverable
Check setRefunded for exact preconditions (confirmed status requirement)
Calculate cumulative locked funds over expected protocol lifetime

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: stakeAmounts[level] per challenge to create first losing edge
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Negative for attacker; cumulative damage to protocol

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative across all challenges where first edge loses

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify no recovery path exists via edge.setRefunded logic

threats.title: DelayBuffer replenishRateInBasis=0 Allows Permanent Buffer Depletion
threats.hypothesis_description: isValidBufferConfig validates that replenishRateInBasis <= BASIS (10000) but does NOT enforce replenishRateInBasis > 0. If configured to 0, the buffer never replenishes after depletion. A single sequencer outage that depletes the buffer permanently shortens the force inclusion window to threshold blocks, making the system permanently more vulnerable to force inclusion attacks.
threats.investigation_rationale: INV-STATE-013 identifies this gap. The calcBuffer function uses (elapsed * replenishRateInBasis) / BASIS for replenishment. With replenishRateInBasis=0, this is always 0. Once depleted to threshold by any unexpected delay, the buffer stays at threshold permanently. The effective force inclusion delay becomes min(threshold, delayBlocks), which could be significantly shorter than intended.
deployment_assumptions: replenishRateInBasis is set to 0 in BufferConfig
threats.config_evidence: DelayBuffer.sol:isValidBufferConfig only checks 'config.replenishRateInBasis <= BASIS' — no lower bound check. common.ts config validation does not validate replenishRateInBasis > 0.

proof_of_signal.pattern: isValidBufferConfig: config.replenishRateInBasis <= BASIS — allows 0. calcBuffer line 50: buffer += (elapsed * replenishRateInBasis) / BASIS — yields 0 when rate is 0.

preconditions: Admin sets replenishRateInBasis to 0 (passes validation)
Any sequencer delay depletes the buffer
No subsequent config change to restore replenishment

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: replenishment = (elapsed * 0) / 10000 = 0 always. Buffer is one-way ratchet to threshold.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: After any buffer depletion event, force inclusion window permanently shortened
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Permanent reduction in sequencer exclusivity window enables easier/faster force inclusion

economic_questions: Do any production deployments set replenishRateInBasis to 0?
What is the threshold value relative to delayBlocks in production?
Can admin recover by updating replenishRateInBasis later?
validation_requirements: Verify isValidBufferConfig accepts 0
Simulate buffer depletion with rate=0 and confirm no recovery
Check deployment scripts for default replenishRateInBasis values

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: permanent force inclusion window reduction

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any sequencer outage triggers permanent depletion

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All L2 users relying on censorship resistance guarantees
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Censorship resistance degradation for entire rollup

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm zero-rate prevents any buffer recovery

threats.title: DelayBuffer Replenishment Rounding Truncation Prevents Buffer Recovery Under Frequent Small Updates
threats.hypothesis_description: The buffer replenishment calculation uses integer division: (elapsed * replenishRateInBasis) / BASIS. For small elapsed values and small replenishRateInBasis, the replenishment rounds down to zero. A sequencer posting frequent small batches (each with small elapsed blocks) could prevent buffer replenishment entirely, keeping the buffer depleted at threshold despite normal operations continuing.
threats.investigation_rationale: If replenishRateInBasis is, say, 100 (1%) and batches are posted every block (elapsed=1), replenishment = (1 * 100) / 10000 = 0. The buffer never recovers. Even with elapsed=99, replenishment = (99 * 100) / 10000 = 0. Only at elapsed=100 does replenishment = 1 block. This means buffer recovery is completely blocked for high-frequency batch posting.
deployment_assumptions: replenishRateInBasis is small (e.g., < 100 basis points) AND batch posting frequency is high
threats.config_evidence: DelayBuffer.sol calcBuffer line 50: buffer += (elapsed * replenishRateInBasis) / BASIS — integer division truncates.

proof_of_signal.pattern: buffer += (elapsed * replenishRateInBasis) / BASIS — with elapsed=1 and rate=100, result is 0 due to integer division truncation

preconditions: replenishRateInBasis * typical_elapsed < BASIS (10000)
High-frequency batch posting (every few blocks)
Buffer has been depleted from a previous delay event

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: For rate=100 (1%): need elapsed >= 100 blocks for any replenishment. If batches every 10 blocks: replenishment = (10 * 100) / 10000 = 0.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Sequencer posts frequent small batches to prevent buffer recovery after depletion
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Keeps force inclusion window at minimum (threshold), facilitating easier force inclusion attacks

economic_questions: What are typical replenishRateInBasis values in production?
What is the average batch posting frequency?
How long would buffer recovery take with rounding vs without?
validation_requirements: Calculate minimum elapsed needed for 1 block of replenishment at various rates
Compare actual batch posting intervals with minimum replenishment threshold
Simulate buffer recovery over 1000 blocks with realistic parameters

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas costs for frequent batch posting (~batch poster operational cost)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Ongoing as long as frequent posting continues

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Extended period of reduced censorship resistance

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: L2 users relying on buffer recovery for censorship resistance
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Depends on duration of suppressed buffer recovery

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: replenishRateInBasis in production, batch posting interval statistics
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compute exact replenishment per batch at various intervals and rates

threats.title: BOLDUpgradeAction Caps Staker Refunds at 50 Potentially Trapping Remaining Staker Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: In BOLDUpgradeAction.cleanupOldRollup, the staker iteration loop is capped at 50 stakers. If a rollup has more than 50 stakers at upgrade time, stakers at index 50+ are not automatically refunded. They must manually withdraw through the IMPL_PATCHED_OLD_ROLLUP_USER logic post-upgrade. If the patched implementation doesn't properly handle their withdrawal (e.g., stakers in active challenges), their funds could be permanently locked.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code explicitly caps at 50: 'if (stakerCount > 50) { stakerCount = 50; }'. INV-STATE-020 identifies this as a potential fund-trapping issue. The patched old rollup user logic must handle all remaining staker scenarios (active challenges, various stake states) to prevent fund loss.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (stakerCount > 50) { stakerCount = 50; } — Hard cap on iteration. Stakers beyond index 50 must self-service via IMPL_PATCHED_OLD_ROLLUP_USER.

preconditions: Rollup has more than 50 stakers at upgrade time
IMPL_PATCHED_OLD_ROLLUP_USER doesn't properly handle all staker states
Stakers beyond index 50 are unaware they need to manually withdraw

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Stakers[50..N] are skipped entirely in the refund loop
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: If patched logic has bugs for edge-case staker states (in-challenge stakers), funds stuck permanently
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No attacker profit; risk of permanent fund lock for unrefunded stakers

economic_questions: How many stakers do production rollups typically have?
Does IMPL_PATCHED_OLD_ROLLUP_USER handle all staker states (including in-challenge)?
What is the total stake value at risk for stakers beyond index 50?
validation_requirements: Verify IMPL_PATCHED_OLD_ROLLUP_USER withdrawal logic for edge cases
Test upgrade with >50 stakers and verify all can withdraw post-upgrade
Check if stakers in active challenges can withdraw via patched logic

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Permanent if patched logic is insufficient

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Potential permanent fund lock for subset of stakers

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Stakers at index >= 50 in the old rollup
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to their full staked amount
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Sum of stakes for stakers beyond index 50

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Typical staker count in production, stake amounts
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify IMPL_PATCHED_OLD_ROLLUP_USER handles all staker states

threats.title: isSynced Check in DelayBuffer Can Cause Underflow Revert After Chain Fork
threats.hypothesis_description: The isSynced function computes block.number - self.prevBlockNumber without safe subtraction. Under Solidity 0.8's checked arithmetic, if prevBlockNumber > block.number (possible after a chain fork where block numbers reset or if a forked chain has lower block numbers), this would revert with an underflow. Since isSynced is called by isDelayProofRequired (which gates batch posting) and isUpdatable (which gates buffer updates), an underflow would brick all batch posting functions that check delay proof requirements.
threats.investigation_rationale: removeDelayAfterFork resets delayBlocks/futureBlocks/delaySeconds/futureSeconds to 1 but does NOT reset buffer state (prevBlockNumber, prevSequencedBlockNumber). If a fork results in lower block numbers on the new chain, the buffer's prevBlockNumber could exceed block.number, causing isSynced to revert and bricking delay-bufferable batch posting.

proof_of_signal.pattern: return block.number - self.prevBlockNumber <= self.threshold; — unchecked subtraction. removeDelayAfterFork at SequencerInbox.sol:196-200 resets delay params but NOT buffer.prevBlockNumber.

preconditions: Chain fork occurs (chainId changes)
New chain has block.number < buffer.prevBlockNumber from old chain
isDelayBufferable is true
removeDelayAfterFork is called (resets delays but not buffer)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: block.number - self.prevBlockNumber underflows when block.number < prevBlockNumber
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: After fork, any batch posting with delay proof check reverts permanently
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit; complete denial of service for batch posting on forked chain

economic_questions: In a fork scenario, how likely is block.number to be lower on the new chain?
Can the admin fix this by calling setBufferConfig to reset buffer state?
Does this permanently brick the delay-bufferable sequencer inbox?
validation_requirements: Simulate fork with lower block numbers and test isSynced
Verify removeDelayAfterFork doesn't reset buffer state
Check if setBufferConfig can recover from this state

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete batch posting failure on delay-bufferable chains

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All L2 users on the forked chain
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete loss of L2 functionality until resolved

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: buffer.prevBlockNumber values, fork scenario block numbers
